title: `SnapshotPolicy::LogsSinceLast` loses a trigger that races an in-flight build and never re-arms
author: SebastianThiebaud
body:
**Describe the bug**

**`SnapshotPolicy::LogsSinceLast` loses a trigger that races an in-flight build and never re-arms, stalling snapshots (and policy-gated purge) on a quiescent node.**

A policy-fired snapshot trigger that arrives while a previous build is still running is silently dropped, but the policy's bookkeeping still records it as an attempt. Nothing re-checks the policy when the in-flight build completes. After a write burst followed by quiescence, a node's snapshot can therefore sit arbitrarily far behind `committed` until `threshold` *further* entries commit, which never happens on an idle cluster. This is despite `committed − snapshot_last ≥ threshold` (the policy's own firing condition) holding the whole time. Everything gated on snapshot progress stalls with it: `max_in_snapshot_log_to_keep` purge (the log grows unboundedly), application-level retention keyed to the snapshot index, and learner installs from the stale snapshot.

The mechanism (all four legs verified at `v0.10.0-alpha.28`; still present on `main` as of 2026-07-10):

1. `RaftCore::trigger_routine_actions` (`openraft/src/core/raft_core.rs:918-921`) advances `self.core_state.snapshot_tried_at = Some(at)` **before** calling `self.trigger_snapshot()`, and `RaftCore::trigger_snapshot` (`raft_core.rs:897-900`) discards the handler's return value.
2. `SnapshotHandler::trigger_snapshot` (`openraft/src/engine/handler/snapshot_handler/mod.rs:31-45`) returns `false` without queueing a build when `io_state.building_snapshot()` is true.
3. `Engine::on_building_snapshot_done` (`openraft/src/engine/engine_impl.rs:706`) clears `building_snapshot` and updates the snapshot meta, but never re-evaluates `should_snapshot`, and `snapshot_tried_at` keeps the phantom attempt.
4. `SnapshotPolicy::should_snapshot` (`openraft/src/config/config.rs:114-136`) computes `base_log_id = max(last_tried_at, snapshot_last_log_id)`, so the recorded-but-dropped attempt suppresses the policy until `committed` advances a full `threshold` past the position the lost trigger was meant to cover.

**To Reproduce**

Deterministic reproduction (single node, all-default type config, `LogsSinceLast(10)`; the state machine is the `examples/sm-mem` shape plus a gate that holds `build_snapshot()` open until released, standing in for any build slow enough to overlap the next `threshold` commits: a large state machine, CPU contention, a slow disk):

1. `git clone https://github.com/SebastianThiebaud/openraft-lost-snapshot-trigger-repro && cd openraft-lost-snapshot-trigger-repro`
2. `cargo run` narrates the wedge and exits 1 while the bug exists (exits 0 once fixed).
3. `cargo test` asserts the expected re-fire after the in-flight build completes; it fails with a `LOST TRIGGER` diagnostic on current versions.

What the harness does: write past the threshold so the policy fires at index 9 and the (held) build starts; keep writing so the policy fires again at index 19, where this second trigger is dropped while `snapshot_tried_at` becomes 19; release the build, which completes at its captured position 9; stop writing. The node now sits at `snapshot = 9`, `committed = 23` forever (`should_snapshot` won't fire until committed reaches 30).

**Expected behavior**

`LogsSinceLast(threshold)` behaves as a level-triggered policy: whenever `committed − snapshot_last ≥ threshold` holds, a snapshot build eventually runs, at the latest when the in-flight build that swallowed the trigger completes. The snapshot converges toward `committed` without requiring further writes or a manual `Raft::trigger().snapshot()`.

**Actual behavior**

The snapshot stays pinned at the pre-burst position for as long as the node is quiescent; a manual trigger (which bypasses the policy bookkeeping) un-wedges it instantly, showing the build machinery itself is healthy:

```text
Wedge established: snapshot=9, committed=23, gap=14 (≥ threshold 10).

 elapsed  committed  last_applied  snapshot  purged
    0.5s         23            23         9       9
    ...                                              (unchanged for the whole window)
   10.2s         23            23         9       9

BUG REPRODUCED: snapshot pinned at 9 while committed = 23 for the whole window,
even though committed − snapshot ≥ threshold held throughout.

Proving liveness is otherwise intact: calling Raft::trigger().snapshot() (bypasses the policy)...
Manual trigger un-wedged it immediately: snapshot 9 → 23.
```

The dropped trigger is visible in openraft's own debug logs (`RUST_LOG=openraft=debug cargo run`):

```text
18:10:41.957776 DEBUG trigger_routine_actions: snapshot policy triggered at: T1-N1.9
18:10:41.957822  INFO trigger_routine_actions:trigger_snapshot:trigger_snapshot: push snapshot building command
18:10:41.974179 DEBUG trigger_routine_actions: snapshot policy triggered at: T1-N1.19
18:10:41.974226 DEBUG trigger_routine_actions:trigger_snapshot:trigger_snapshot: snapshot building is in progress, do not trigger snapshot
18:10:41.981374  INFO on_building_snapshot_done: snapshot_meta: {snapshot_id: T1-N1-9-1, last_log:T1-N1.9, ...}
        ... 10 seconds of silence: the policy never fires again ...
18:10:52.019711  INFO handle_api_msg:trigger_snapshot:trigger_snapshot: push snapshot building command   <- manual Raft::trigger().snapshot()
18:10:52.020363  INFO on_building_snapshot_done: snapshot_meta: {snapshot_id: T1-N1-23-2, last_log:T1-N1.23, ...}
```

**Env (please complete the following information):**

- Openraft version: first hit on `v0.10.0-alpha.25`; reproduced identically on `v0.10.0-alpha.28` (latest published; the repro pins it).
- Does the bug still exist in the latest version? Yes: reproduced on `v0.10.0-alpha.28`, and all four code legs above are present on `main` as of 2026-07-10 (`raft_core.rs` advances `snapshot_tried_at` before the discarded `trigger_snapshot()`; the handler still silently returns `false` mid-build).
- Rust-toolchain: `cargo 1.94.0 (85eff7c80 2026-01-15)`
- OS: macOS 26.5.1 (the mechanism is platform-independent single-node engine logic)
- CPU: Apple M4 Max (arm64)

**Additional files:**

- Logs: excerpt above; full logs via `RUST_LOG=openraft=debug cargo run 2>debug.log` in the repro repo.

**Additional context**

We hit this in production-shaped integration tests under full-workspace CPU load (a build overlapped a 15-entry burst at `threshold = 5`); the wedge presents as a *stable* metrics signature that no wait budget recovers: `snapshot_last_log_id` pinned while `last_applied`/`committed` sit far ahead. We currently work around it with a small level-triggered watchdog that re-issues `Raft::trigger().snapshot()` while `last_applied − snapshot_last ≥ threshold` holds with no snapshot progress.

Two fix directions that would restore level-triggered liveness:

- Only advance `snapshot_tried_at` when `SnapshotHandler::trigger_snapshot` actually queued a build. The handler already returns that bool; `trigger_routine_actions` just has to use it. This looks minimal: the attempt bookkeeping then can't outrun the builds that actually happen, and the next routine check after build completion re-fires naturally.
- Alternatively, treat build completion as a policy edge: in `on_building_snapshot_done`, clear the stale `snapshot_tried_at` and re-run the `should_snapshot` check. (Note: re-checking alone without clearing `snapshot_tried_at` is not sufficient, because the phantom attempt would still suppress the policy.)

Happy to provide more details or test against a patch. Also, the repro's `cargo test` is written to pass once the re-arm is fixed. I can also help providing a fix / PR once we align on the direction.

title: `ProgressEntry::next_send` never sends a snapshot to a follower behind a fully‑purged log
author: AustenSchunk

title: Hung follower RPC can block raft core loop
author: jkosh44
body:
**Describe the bug**
If a `ReplicationCore` task is stuck in `NetStreamAppend::stream_append` (due to a half closed connection or some other reason), and that replica is removed from the membership, then the `RaftCore` main task will be stuck in `RaftCore::close_replication` and not make any progress.

**To Reproduce**
Steps to reproduce the behavior:
1. Checkout https://github.com/jkosh44/openraft/commit/4ab55e6310e3fe1efab6114bdea3387285f07169
2. `cargo test --package tests --test membership -- t51_hung_follower_blocks_loop::remove_hung_follower_must_not_block_raft_core_loop`

**Expected behavior**
Either the RPC eventually times out or the main `RaftCore` tasked is not blocked by the RPC.

**Actual behavior**
The RPC never times out (or times out after a long time) and the `RaftCore` task is blocked on the RPC.

**Env:**
- Openraft version: 41d3a30de44d64fb44d64a7e98e9ef3a50a90d08
- Does the bug still there in the latest version? Yes
- Rust-toolchain: cargo 1.96.0-nightly (f298b8c82 2026-02-24)
- OS: Ubuntu 24.04.4 LTS
- CPU: Intel® Core™ i7-10510U

**Additional context**
Here is a more in depth analysis of what is happening.

#### `RaftCore` Task

1. A follower is removed which eventually calls [`ReplicationHandler::rebuild_replication_streams`](https://github.com/databendlabs/openraft/blob/275cce95b6c9ca87021757b821670b9e598f4d0b/openraft/src/engine/handler/replication_handler/mod.rs#L325-L352). This pushes the command `Command::RebuildReplicationStreams`.
2. The `RaftCore` [main loop](https://github.com/databendlabs/openraft/blob/275cce95b6c9ca87021757b821670b9e598f4d0b/openraft/src/core/raft_core.rs#L1129-L1133) will eventually pop and run this command.
3. The [handling of `Command::RebuildReplicationStreams`](https://github.com/databendlabs/openraft/blob/275cce95b6c9ca87021757b821670b9e598f4d0b/openraft/src/core/raft_core.rs#L2422-L2460) calls `RaftCore::close_replication` passing in a removed `ReplicationHandle`.
4. [The `RaftCore::close_replication` implementation](https://github.com/databendlabs/openraft/blob/275cce95b6c9ca87021757b821670b9e598f4d0b/openraft/src/core/raft_core.rs#L2199-L2212) awaits the join handle from `ReplicationHandle`. Note the join handle is a handle to a task running `ReplicationCore::main`.
5. At this point the main loop will not make ANY progress until the join handle is fully awaited and the task has completed.

#### `ReplicationCore` Task

As part of the [main `ReplicationCore` task loop](https://github.com/databendlabs/openraft/blob/275cce95b6c9ca87021757b821670b9e598f4d0b/openraft/src/replication/mod.rs#L200-L307), the task [calls `NetStreamAppend::stream_append`](https://github.com/databendlabs/openraft/blob/275cce95b6c9ca87021757b821670b9e598f4d0b/openraft/src/replication/mod.rs#L271). This task will not complete until this method returns. If this method hangs for some reason (due to network issues, etc), then this task will hang. If the `RaftCore` main loop is waiting on this task (as described above), then that will hang as well.

Both [`RaftNetworkV2::append_entries`](https://github.com/databendlabs/openraft/blob/275cce95b6c9ca87021757b821670b9e598f4d0b/openraft/src/network/v2/network.rs#L96-L101) and [`NetStreamAppend::stream_append`](https://github.com/databendlabs/openraft/blob/275cce95b6c9ca87021757b821670b9e598f4d0b/openraft/src/network/stream_append_trait.rs#L55-L61) accept an [`RPCOption`](https://github.com/databendlabs/openraft/blob/275cce95b6c9ca87021757b821670b9e598f4d0b/openraft/src/network/rpc_option.rs#L3-L16), which contains a `hard_ttl: Duration` field, but neither mention that the implementer needs to implement a timeout. Also the [docs of `RpcOption`](https://github.com/databendlabs/openraft/blob/275cce95b6c9ca87021757b821670b9e598f4d0b/openraft/src/network/rpc_option.rs#L27-L49) claim that openraft will enforce the hard TTL

>         once the
>        /// `hard_ttl()` ends, Openraft will terminate the ongoing RPC at once.

>        /// When exceeding this limit, the RPC will be dropped by Openraft at once.

However, as far as I can tell, this TTL is not enforced by openraft anywhere. Also when looking at the examples in the repo, none of them implement either the soft TTL or the hard TTL.

- https://github.com/databendlabs/openraft/blob/275cce95b6c9ca87021757b821670b9e598f4d0b/examples/raft-kv-memstore-grpc/src/network/mod.rs#L124-L128
- https://github.com/databendlabs/openraft/blob/275cce95b6c9ca87021757b821670b9e598f4d0b/examples/network-v1-http/src/lib.rs#L171-L176
- https://github.com/databendlabs/openraft/blob/275cce95b6c9ca87021757b821670b9e598f4d0b/examples/raft-kv-memstore-single-threaded/src/network.rs#L30-L34
- https://github.com/databendlabs/openraft/blob/275cce95b6c9ca87021757b821670b9e598f4d0b/benchmarks/minimal/src/network.rs#L105-L109

title: Snapshot install can retain a stale `effective` membership, producing disjoint quorums and lost committed entries
author: AustenSchunk
body:
**openraft version:** 0.10.0-alpha.25 (git rev 41d3a30de44d64fb44d64a7e98e9ef3a50a90d08)

### Summary

When a node installs a snapshot whose `last_log_id` supersedes (and purges) an **uncommitted** membership entry that is currently the node's `effective` membership, openraft keeps the stale `effective` membership instead of resetting it to the snapshot's `last_membership`. The decision appears to be index-based: the stale entry has a *higher index* than the snapshot's membership entry, so it is treated as "newer" and retained — even though the snapshot's `last_log_id` has a higher term and has already purged that entry from the log.

The node is then left believing it is a **voter** in a configuration that has actually been superseded (where it is only a **learner**). It can campaign and win an election counting a quorum of that phantom configuration, which may be **disjoint** from the quorum that committed entries under the real configuration — overwriting committed log entries.

### Reproduction conditions

Requires rapid membership churn plus snapshotting on a lagging node:

1. Node B, while briefly leader in term `T`, appends an **uncommitted** membership entry `M_stale` at log index `i` (e.g. voters `{A,B,C}`) and sets it as its `effective` membership.
2. B loses leadership before `M_stale` commits. The cluster, under later leaders, commits a **different** membership `M_committed` (e.g. voters `{A,C,D}`, B demoted to learner) whose membership log id is at index `j < i` but term `> T`, and advances the log far beyond `i`.
3. B falls behind and, instead of catching up via `AppendEntries` (which would detect the conflict at index `i`, truncate, and revert `effective` to the committed membership), receives an **`InstallSnapshot`** whose `last_log_id` is at index `≫ i` and term `> T`, and whose `last_membership = M_committed` (index `j`).

After the install, B's state is internally inconsistent:

- `committed_membership = M_committed @ (term T+, index j)`  ✅ (taken from the snapshot)
- `effective_membership = M_stale @ (term T, index i)`  ❌ (should have been dropped)
- `purged_upto ≥ index i` — i.e. the entry backing `effective` no longer exists in the log.

Note `i > j` but `term(i) < term(j)`: a higher index with a lower term is impossible in a single consistent log, which is the tell-tale of the retained stale entry.

### Consequence (the safety break)

Because B's `effective` membership says it is a voter in `M_stale`:

- B triggers and wins an election in a new term, computing the quorum over `M_stale` (e.g. votes from `{A,B}`, where A grants on log-recency grounds while lagging).
- Entries had been committed under `M_committed` by a quorum (e.g. `{C,D}`).
- `{A,B} ∩ {C,D} = ∅`. The new leader (B) lacks the committed entries and replicates its shorter log, **truncating committed (and already-applied) entries on the followers that had them**.

This manifests as a follower's committed index moving backwards within a single process lifetime (no crash/restart) — i.e. loss of committed entries.

### Root cause

In the follower snapshot-install path (`FollowingHandler::install_full_snapshot` → membership reconciliation), when reconciling the snapshot's `last_membership` with the current `effective` membership, the code keeps whichever membership has the greater **index**. After `InstallSnapshot`, the log is purged up to `snapshot.last_log_id`, so any `effective` membership entry at an index `≤ snapshot.last_log_id` has been removed from the log and is no longer authoritative — regardless of whether its index is greater than the snapshot's `last_membership` index. The snapshot's `last_log_id` (a durable, committed point) should be treated as authoritative, and an `effective` membership derived from a purged/superseded entry must be reset to the snapshot's `last_membership`.

### Suggested fix

In the snapshot-install membership reconciliation, do not retain the existing `effective` membership when its log id is at or below `snapshot.meta.last_log_id` (i.e. it falls within the range purged by the install). Concretely:

- After installing a snapshot, if `effective_membership.log_id <= snapshot.last_log_id`, set both `effective` and `committed` membership to `snapshot.last_membership`.
- Only retain a distinct `effective` membership across a snapshot install if it is backed by a log entry strictly **after** `snapshot.last_log_id` (a genuinely newer, still-present membership entry).

Equivalently: gate the "keep the higher-index membership" comparison on the entry still existing in the log after purge, rather than on index alone. This mirrors the `AppendEntries` conflict path, which already reverts the `effective` membership to the last committed one when the backing log entry is truncated.

### Severity

Consensus safety violation (lost committed entries / two leaders' logs diverging at committed indices). It only surfaces under the specific ordering above (uncommitted membership entry on a node that then catches up via snapshot rather than log replication), so it is rare in steady state but reachable under heavy membership reconfiguration combined with snapshot-based catch-up.

title: leader transfer panics if target node is not a voter
author: AustenSchunk

title: Commit index can increase without actual quorum
author: AustenSchunk

title: Pre-Vote deadlock during membership changes
author: AustenSchunk

title: Leader read failure causes corrupted follower log
author: AustenSchunk
body:
Hello, we are currently validating correctness of our openraft-based implementation and see an issue where the leader can build an inconsistent `AppendEntries` (`prev_log_id = None` with entries that don't start at index 0) after a log-read error during replication, corrupting a follower's log.

## Environment
- openraft 0.10.21.alpha (git rev `0f6bb9a`)
- Single Raft group, 5 voters
- Custom `RaftLogStorage` whose log enforces **contiguity** (rejects/asserts on a gap between the last stored index and the first appended index). openraft's reference `memstore`/`rocksstore` do **not** enforce this, so the bug is silent there.

## Summary
Under a specific combination of (a) the leader having purged a log prefix, (b) a follower that is far behind, and (c) a transient storage **read** error on the leader during replication, the leader can emit an `AppendEntries` RPC where `prev_log_id = None` but the attached `entries` start at an index well above 0. Such an RPC violates the core invariant `entries.first().index == prev_log_id.index + 1` (and `== 0` when `prev_log_id` is `None`). A follower applies it (because `prev_log_id = None` trivially passes the log-matching check), truncates, and appends the entries non-contiguously — leaving a gap in its log. Stores that tolerate gaps (the reference stores) silently accept this; stores that enforce log contiguity fail hard.

## Preconditions / setup
1. Leader `L` (term T) has taken a snapshot and **purged its log up to index `P`**; its persisted log now physically begins at `P+1`. openraft's `RaftState` correctly reports `purged = Some(LogId(.., P))`.
2. Follower `F` is significantly behind — its `matching` on the leader is `None` (or below `P`) — and is reachable only intermittently (e.g., a partition is dropping RPCs).
3. While `L` is probing/replicating to `F`, a transient storage error is returned by the leader's `RaftLogReader` (e.g., `limited_get_log_entries(..)` returns `Err`).

## Observed behavior (timeline)
1. `L` begins replicating to `F`. Because `F`'s `matching` is `None` and `L`'s log starts at `P+1`, openraft correctly probes with `prev_log_id = Some(LogId(.., P))` and `entries = [P+1 ..= last]`. (Correct — `prev` is clamped to the purged boundary.) These RPCs are repeatedly dropped by the partition.
2. A storage read error fires while `L` is reading its own log to build the replication payload for `F`.
3. **After the error, `L` resumes replication to `F` with `prev_log_id` reset from `Some(LogId(.., P))` to `None`** — i.e., it now intends to send from the very beginning and reads its own log for the range `[0, last+1)`, which spans the purged prefix `[0, P]`.
4. The reader, behaving per the de-facto contract (return the entries that intersect the requested range; openraft's own `memstore` does `log.range(0..last+1)` over a `BTreeMap`), returns an **offset slice starting at `P+1`** rather than at index 0.
5. In `read_log_entries` (`openraft/src/replication/stream_state.rs`), openraft builds the sending range as `LogIdRange::new(rng.prev.clone(), Some(last))` — it keeps the **requested** `prev` (`None`) and derives only `last` from the returned entries. It does **not** verify that the first returned entry's index equals `prev_log_id.next_index()`. (The nearby `debug_assert!` only checks the entry *count*, and is compiled out in release builds.)
6. `L` sends `AppendEntries { prev_log_id: None, entries: [P+1 ..= last] }`. This RPC reaches `F`.
7. On `F`, `prev_log_id = None` passes the consistency check unconditionally; `F` truncates and appends `entries` starting at `P+1`. If `F`'s log ends at `L_f < P`, indices `L_f+1 .. P` are now missing → a hole in `F`'s log.

## Expected behavior
The leader must never emit an `AppendEntries` whose first entry index is greater than `prev_log_id.index + 1` (or `> 0` when `prev_log_id` is `None`). Concretely, one or more of:
- The leader should not request log entries below its own `purged` point; the replication `prev` should be clamped to the purged log id (as it already is on the non-error path), or
- When the follower's required position is at/below the leader's purged/snapshot boundary, replication should transition to **snapshot** transfer rather than log entries, and/or
- `read_log_entries` should validate that the first entry returned by the reader has index `prev_log_id.next_index()`; if it doesn't (i.e., the requested range spanned purged indices and the store returned an offset slice — a legitimate reader behavior), openraft should not construct a log-append RPC from it.

## Actual root cause (two contributing defects)
1. **Loss of the purged clamp after a replication read error.** On the happy path the leader correctly probes `F` with `prev = Some(purged)`. A transient `RaftLogReader` error during replication causes the leader to resume with `prev = None`, i.e. below its own purged boundary, requesting purged entries.
2. **No validation of the returned slice in `read_log_entries`.** openraft pairs the requested `prev_log_id` with whatever entries the reader returns, trusting them to begin exactly at `prev.next_index()`. Since returning an offset slice for a range spanning purged indices is consistent with how openraft's own reference store behaves, a reader is within its rights to return `[P+1 ..]` for a `[0 ..]` request — and openraft turns that into a malformed RPC.

## Why the reference stores hide this
`memstore`/`rocksstore` store the log in a gap-tolerant structure (`BTreeMap`/key-value insert by index), so appending `[P+1 ..]` onto a log ending at `L_f < P` just inserts the keys and leaves a logical gap with no error. The malformed RPC therefore produces silent log corruption in the reference stores and is never surfaced by the existing test suite. A `RaftLogStorage` that enforces contiguity (e.g., one backed by a WAL that asserts the first appended index is `last + 1`) rejects the append outright, which is how this was discovered.

## Impact
- Safety: a follower's log can be left with a gap (non-contiguous entries), which is a Raft log-invariant violation.
- For contiguity-enforcing stores this manifests as a hard failure (append rejected / panic) on the follower.
- Trigger requires a storage read error coinciding with replication to a lagging follower while the leader has a purged prefix — plausible in production with real disks and snapshotting, and readily hit under fault-injection/deterministic-simulation testing.

## Suggested fix areas
- `openraft/src/replication/stream_state.rs` — in `read_log_entries`, assert/verify `entries.first().index == prev_log_id.next_index()` and bail out of log-append construction (fall through to snapshot replication) when it doesn't hold.
- The replication progress recomputation after a `Notification::StorageError`/read error — ensure the replication `prev` for a target remains clamped to the leader's purged log id (never resets to `None` below `purged`), and that a target whose position is at/below the purged boundary is routed to snapshot transfer.

title: Split snapshot data installation from vote-based leadership assertion
author: drmingdrmer
body:
## Problem

OpenRaft currently checks the sender vote before installing a received snapshot.

That couples two different concerns:

- Whether the snapshot data is useful to the local state machine.
- Whether the sender is still a valid leader.

A snapshot contains committed and applied state machine data. The state machine can decide whether to install or skip it by comparing the snapshot `last_log_id` with local state. This decision does not need the sender to be the current leader.

## Proposal

Handle `InstallSnapshot` in two steps.

First, transfer the snapshot to the node and ask the state machine worker to handle it.

The state machine worker decides whether the input snapshot should be installed or skipped based on the snapshot metadata.

- If the snapshot is newer, install it.
- If the snapshot is stale, skip it.
- If the state machine returns an I/O error, fail the request.

A skipped snapshot is still a positive data-transfer result. It means the follower already contains the data covered by the input snapshot.

Second, check the vote on the follower.

After the state machine worker handles the snapshot, the follower checks the sender's vote. `InstallSnapshot` still works as a leadership assertion, similar to `AppendEntries`.

If the follower has seen a higher vote, it returns that higher vote to the sender. The sender should then step down or stop treating itself as the latest leader.

## API Behavior

At the Raft API level, the state machine should explicitly report whether the snapshot was installed or skipped.

For a skipped snapshot, the result should include the newer local `last_log_id` that already contains the input snapshot.

Both `Installed` and `Skipped` should be treated as successful snapshot handling. Only state machine I/O errors should fail the data-transfer part.

## Semantics

Snapshot data handling:

- Newer snapshot: install and return success.
- Stale snapshot: skip and return success with local newer `last_log_id`.
- I/O error: return error.

Leadership assertion:

- Always check vote after snapshot handling.
- If the sender vote is stale, return the follower's higher vote.
- If the sender vote is current, the sender may treat the response as successful replication.

## Motivation

Snapshot transfer is state-machine data movement. It should be guarded by snapshot freshness.

Vote checking answers a different question: whether the sender is still a valid leader. That check should remain, but it should not prevent the node from using valid committed snapshot data.

This keeps snapshot installation independent from log replication while preserving stale-leader detection.

title: Apply-worker debug-assert panics on a contract-compliant short read from entries_stream
author: PeterKnego
body:
## Summary

The state-machine worker's apply path contains a `debug_assertions`-only invariant:

```rust
// openraft/src/core/sm/worker.rs — Worker::apply()
self.state_machine.apply(Box::pin(strm)).await.sto_apply(last.clone())?;

#[cfg(debug_assertions)]
{
    assert_eq!(end - 1, got_last_index.load(std::sync::atomic::Ordering::Relaxed));
}
```

`got_last_index` is the index of the last entry pulled from the stream returned by
`RaftLogReader::entries_stream(since..end)`. The assert requires the stream to yield an
entry at `end - 1` (`= last.index()`).

This is **stricter than the documented contract of `RaftLogReader::try_get_log_entries`**,
which the default `entries_stream` is built on:

> If the log doesn't contain all the requested entries, return the existing entries.
> **The absence of an entry is tolerated only at the beginning or end of the range.**
> Missing entries within the range (i.e., holes) are not permitted and should result in an error.

So a **contract-compliant** log reader may legally return a short read that omits the entry
at the end of the range. When it does, a fully-draining `RaftStateMachine::apply` returns
`Ok`, but `got_last_index < end - 1`, and the apply worker **panics** — crashing the
state-machine worker task and wedging the node — even though neither the state machine nor
the log reader violated its documented contract.

## Observed impact

Intermittent apply-worker panics during bootstrap and post-partition leader election, when a
freshly-flushed final committed entry is observed late by the reader handle (a read-after-flush
visibility window in the storage implementation). Not a linearizability violation; the node
simply dies on the debug assert. Storage impls with eventually-consistent read/write paths hit
this; a strictly-consistent in-memory store does not.

## Reproduction

Two deterministic reproducers (gated on `debug_assertions`, since the assert is debug-only):

- **Unit** — `openraft/src/core/sm/worker.rs`, `mod apply_drain_assert_tests`: a log reader
  that omits the last entry of the requested range + a fully-draining state machine →
  `apply` panics at the assert (`left: 2, right: 1`). A control test with a full read applies
  cleanly.
- **End-to-end** — `tests/tests/apply_drain_assert_e2e_test.rs`: a real single-node `Raft`
  whose store performs one legal short read on the apply path; a normal `client_write` then
  trips the assert in the running apply worker (`left: 2, right: 0`).

Branch with both tests: <https://github.com/PeterKnego/openraft/tree/fix/apply-drain-assert-short-read>

```
cargo test -p openraft --lib apply_drain_assert_tests
cargo test -p tests   --test apply_drain_assert_e2e_test
```

Both are mutation-checked: disabling the assert makes both fail, confirming they bind to this
exact invariant.

## The real question (why this isn't just "remove the assert")

The assert is over-strict *relative to the `entries_stream` API contract*, but it is also
guarding a genuine correctness requirement: the entries in `[since, end-1]` are **committed**,
so they must exist and be readable. On a short read, the SM never applied the committed entry
at `end-1`, yet the worker reports `ApplyResult.last_applied = last` to `RaftCore`. **Simply
relaxing/removing the assert would make openraft believe an entry is applied when it isn't —
silent under-apply / state divergence**, which is worse than the panic.

So the apply path is using a short-read-*tolerant* read API for data it requires to be
*complete*, and handles the resulting mismatch with a debug panic.

## Possible directions (seeking maintainer guidance)

1. **Fail cleanly instead of panicking:** treat a short read on the committed apply range as a
   `StorageError` (loud, no data loss; `RaftCore` can retry/go fatal) rather than a debug
   `assert_eq!`.
2. **Retry the apply read:** if a short read on the apply range is considered a transient
   visibility condition, re-fetch `entries_stream(since..end)` until complete.
3. **Clarify the contract:** document that, unlike general `try_get_log_entries` reads, the
   apply read requires the full `[since, end)` range (committed entries) and that a short read
   there is a storage violation.

Happy to turn the reproducer branch into a PR for whichever direction you prefer.

## Version

Present on current `main` and `0.10.0-alpha.21`. (`#[cfg(debug_assertions)]` only.)

title: Pre-Vote support / intended handling of the disruptive-voter (term churn) problem in 0.10
author: diptanu
body:
Hi — thanks for openraft. I'm building on `0.10.0-alpha.21` (HTTP/reqwest transport, custom fjall-backed log + state machine) and want to confirm my understanding of the election design, since I couldn't find Pre-Vote in the source.

**Context.** I hit a scenario where a follower that was stopped, missed a backlog of writes, then restarted on the same address became a perpetual higher-term `Candidate` (term climbing 1→7, `last_log` stuck) while the leader stayed at the old term and never stepped down.

**Resolution on my side.** This turned out to be **my transport bug**, not openraft: the still-running leader was reusing a dead pooled keep-alive connection to the restarted node, so `AppendEntries` never landed and the follower campaigned out of starvation. Setting `pool_max_idle_per_host(0)` on the reqwest client fixed it — the follower now rejoins and catches up in a few seconds. So this is **not a bug report**; I'd just like to validate the design model:

1. **Pre-Vote:** Is Pre-Vote implemented in 0.10? (I found none in the source.) Is it planned / on the roadmap? (I see #167 "continuous electing" as related.)
2. **Disruptive voter:** Without Pre-Vote, is the leader-lease mechanism — a node won't grant a vote within `leader_lease`, and won't elect itself until `leader_lease + election_timeout` after perceiving a leader — considered the *complete* intended mitigation for a voter that repeatedly times out and bumps its term? Are there failure modes where a continuously-flapping voter can still force term churn / disrupt a healthy leader?
3. **Rejoin model:** Is the intended recovery for a returned follower simply that the leader's ongoing replication/heartbeat reaches it before its election timer fires (so it never campaigns)? i.e. is "keep the leader reliably reaching the follower" the right mental model, rather than expecting the leader to step down for the higher-term candidate?

Config in use: `heartbeat_interval=250ms`, `election_timeout=600–1200ms`. Happy to move this to Discussions if that's the better venue. Thanks!

title: `RaftCore` panics in `apply_to_state_machine` after follower lag / network delay
author: ethe
body:
We are seeing a reproducible RaftCore panic with `openraft 0.10.0-alpha.20` under network delay chaos.

## Environment

- OpenRaft: `0.10.0-alpha.20`
- Runtime: tokio
- Cluster: 3 voters, 6 independent Raft groups
- Log store: in-memory
- Workload: continuous client writes
- Fault: temporary `tc netem delay` on one node

## What happened

During a `cluster_netem_delay` fault on node 3, two Raft groups on that node permanently stopped making progress.

The process stayed alive, but the affected RaftCore tasks were panicked. Other nodes kept retrying `AppendEntries` / `FullSnapshot`, and node 3 kept returning `panicked`.

Relevant log:

```text
2026-06-02T06:39:41Z thread 'ursula-core-1' panicked at openraft-0.10.0-alpha.20/src/core/raft_core.rs:996:63:
called `Option::unwrap()` on a `None` value

2026-06-02T06:39:42Z thread 'ursula-core-0' panicked at openraft-0.10.0-alpha.20/src/core/raft_core.rs:996:63:
called `Option::unwrap()` on a `None` value
```

After that:

OpenRaft AppendEntries: panicked
OpenRaft install snapshot: panicked

The affected replicas stayed stuck:

group 2 / node 3: committed=6352, applied=6352
group 5 / node 3: committed=6593, applied=6593

while peers advanced past 100k entries.

## Suspected cause

The panic is here:

```rust
let log_id = self.engine.state.get_log_id(*index).unwrap();
```

in `RaftCore::apply_to_state_machine()`.

It looks like `client_responders` can retain a responder for an index whose log id is no longer present in `engine.state.log_ids`, likely after follower lag plus truncate/purge/snapshot progress under leadership changes or delayed replication.

I noticed `Command::TruncateLog` drains affected `client_responders`, but `Command::PurgeLog` only updates the log store / purged index and does not clear responders covered by the purge. I am not sure this is the exact path, but the panic requires that combination: pending responder exists, `get_log_id(index)` returns None.

## Expected behavior

`RaftCore` should not panic here. If a responder refers to a log index that was truncated/purged/superseded, it should be completed with an error or ignored safely, rather than killing the `RaftCore` task.

## Notes

Our application log store returns `last_log_id == last_purged_log_id` when all entries are purged, so the empty-log boundary follows the documented OpenRaft storage contract.

title: Debounce Window for Api Message Receiver
author: manuelgdlvh
body:
**Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe.**
In slow or busy networks, sending messages one by one causes a big performance penalty. I would like to add support for a debounce window in the Msg Receiver and make the batch size configurable (it is currently hardcoded). By default, the debounce window will be set to zero, but it can be configured to allow more time for batching.

**Describe the solution you'd like**
The solution is to make the Raft Batch Message Receiver configurable with:

* batch size
* linger time in milliseconds

This will allow the receiver to wait for a short time before sending messages, increasing the number of batched records and improving throughput in high-latency connections.

Default values:
* batch size: 1024
* linger ms: 0

**Describe alternatives you've considered**
N/A

**Additional context**
Module affected -> merged_raft_msg_receiver.rs
Method affected -> merge_client_writes()

title: transfer_leader + ensure_linearizable Stops Writes Indefinitely
author: zhixinwen
body:
**Describe the bug**
`transfer_leader` can stop writes indefinitely when transfer target never takes over; `ensure_linearizable(ReadIndex)` prevents new election.

**Summary**
We observed a stuck leadership-transfer state where:
the source leader keeps returning `ForwardToLeader(target)` for all writes,
reads using `ensure_linearizable(ReadIndex)` continue to succeed, this can persist for hours without self-healing.

From behavior and code reading, this appears to be due to [transfer_to](https://github.com/databendlabs/openraft/blob/09fa789e3aebd9ce250616ab7d24b8dc14f9d70f/openraft/src/proposer/leader.rs#L167) being sticky on the source leader with no timeout/abort path, combined with read-index traffic refreshing follower leases and preventing follower elections. When this field is set, all writes would fail with `ForwardToLeader` error. openraft stops heartbeats when this field is set. However `ensure_linearizable(ReadIndex)` would not check this field and continue to succeed, it would also send heartbeats, therefore new election would not happen.

**Expected behavior**
At least one of the following should happen automatically:
transfer mode should have a timeout/abort path on source and return to writable leader behavior if transfer cannot complete, or
linearizable read path should respect transfer mode and avoid sending lease-refreshing probes that block election-based recovery, or
some built-in recovery path should guarantee transfer mode cannot persist indefinitely without term change.

**Current Workaround**
One idea I have is to wait for the leader to become follower for some time. If it did not happen, trigger an election by call `elect`. But I am not sure if it would have other edge cases, like `elect` fails to send vote request for some reason and no new leader is elected. In this case `transfer_to` is not reset. I am not sure if it is a valid edge case though.

```
                      group
                        .trigger()
                        .transfer_leader(target)
                        .await?;
                    if let Err(e) = current_group
                        .group
                        .wait(Some(LEADERSHIP_TRANSFER_TIMEOUT))
                        .state(openraft::ServerState::Follower, "transfer_leader")
                        .await
                    {
                        // The transfer never completed, which means openraft has
                        // `engine.leader.transfer_to = Some(target)` set on this node.
                        // It will reject every client_write with `ForwardToLeader`
                        // until the term changes. Force a new election to bump the
                        // term and rebuild the leader state with `transfer_to = None`.
                        current_group.group.trigger().elect().await?
                    }
```

**Env (please complete the following information):**
- Openraft version 0.10.0-alpha.15
- Does the bug still there in the latest version? yes
- CPU:  x86_64

**Additional files:**
- Logs: Attach logs generate by unittest(e.g., `./openraft/_log/*`), or logs output by examples(`examples/raft-kv-*/test-cluster.sh` will print logs in `n1.log`, `n2.log`, `n3.log`)
- Cargo.lock: [e.g. `./openraft/Cargo.lock`, `./examples/raft-kv-memstore/Cargo.lock`]

**Additional context**
Add any other context about the problem here.

title: Make clap dependency optional
author: svix-jplatte
body:
As far as I can tell, both the `clap` and `byte-unit` dependencies are only there to allow the `Config` type to be parsed from command-line arguments. I would send a simple PR, but due to the `impl Default for Config` it's not entirely trivial.

Would you be open to a PR that makes clap and byte-unit optional and either

- duplicates the default values from the clap attributes into the `Default` implementation or
- adds a custom macro for the `Default` trait that takes the clap attributes into account or
- removes support for parsing `Config` from CLI args entirely (it doesn't appear to be used in this repo)?

title: Backoff is never cleared within a stream replication session after successful RPCs
author: AustenSchunk
body:
Background:

We are on [openraft 0.10-alpha.15](https://docs.rs/openraft/0.10.0-alpha.15/openraft/). We have noticed that we can receive transient `Unavailable` errors which we convert into an `Unreachable` response in the append_entries replication stream. When this occurs, we will see a delay of ~200ms before then next `append_entries` is sent. We believe due to backoff. However, even after successful append_entries calls we continue to see the qps dropped and large batches being sent. This persists indefinitely until the a cancellation event is received (resetting the stream).

We are still investigation the issue and there could be more going on (related to our specific environment). However, this is what we believe is happening:

When replication encounters RPC errors, the backoff mechanism is correctly enabled — enable_backoff creates a Backoff iterator and stores it in a shared Arc<Mutex<Option<Backoff>>>. However, when subsequent RPCs succeed, the Backoff object is never cleared within the stream session. This causes a 200ms sleep (default backoff interval) to be inserted before every AppendEntriesRequest for the remainder of the stream session, even though all RPCs are succeeding.

Root cause:

The backoff state is managed by two independent mechanisms that don't coordinate:

backoff_rank (a counter on ReplicationCore) — reset to 0 on success inside handle_response_stream: https://github.com/databendlabs/openraft/blob/main/openraft/src/replication/mod.rs#L294

The Backoff object (in Arc<Mutex<Option<Backoff>>>, shared between ReplicationCore and StreamState) — only cleared by disable_backoff(), which is called from the outer main loop: https://github.com/databendlabs/openraft/blob/main/openraft/src/replication/mod.rs#L209-L213

In LogsSince (pipeline/stream) mode, the outer loop does not run while a stream session is active — handle_response_stream processes responses in a while let loop while the request stream concurrently generates requests. disable_backoff is never called until the stream session ends.

Meanwhile, StreamState::backoff_if_enabled checks only the Backoff object, not backoff_rank: https://github.com/databendlabs/openraft/blob/main/openraft/src/replication/stream_state.rs#L186-L207

So after a transient error spike, once errors stop:

backoff_rank is reset to 0 on the first successful response
The Backoff object remains Some(...) indefinitely
Every subsequent request sleeps for the backoff duration (default 200ms) before being sent
This persists until something externally breaks the stream session (e.g., network error, cancellation, higher vote)

title: Bug: Log up-to-date check in handle_vote_req uses CommittedLeaderId ordering, violating Leader Completeness in advanced LeaderId mode
author: mengxuroblox

title: grpc example does not implement `send_with_forwarding` - NetworkFactory should be exposed through `Raft`
author: gtema
body:
**Describe the bug**

The grpc example work on it's own, but when the functional test is modified to write the data (application data, not the cluster mgmt) not to the first node (which is apparently magically always a leader) it fails. The http example implements send_with_forwarding method which is trying to address the issue by processing the ForwardToLeader error and resend the message.

Unfortunately the internal NetworkFactory is not exposed through the 'Raft' so that implementing it manually on the service side (the service itself connects to the leader node and writes the request) is not easily possible and requires manual implementation of something similar to the NetworkFactory inside the service for managing connection pool. Sadly the NetworkFactory also defines `new_client` as mutable operation requiring additional RwLock handling. When instead the networkfactory would be exposed through `Raft` (without requiring mutability) the service implementation on the node would be able to forward request. The same would also help for implementing linearizable requests internally (the voter/learner node itself connects to the leader to obtain the read_log_id and waits for the current node to catch up).

**To Reproduce**
Steps to reproduce the behavior:
1. Go to https://github.com/databendlabs/openraft/blob/main/examples/raft-kv-memstore-grpc/tests/test_cluster.rs
2. Initialize the client2 for connecting to the second node and write the new value with the client2
3. 'cargo t'

**Expected behavior**
new value be successfully written

**Actual behavior**
called `Result::unwrap()` on an `Err` value: status: Internal, message: "Failed to write to store: has to forward request to: Some(1), Some(Node { node_id: 1, rpc_addr: \"127.0.0.1:21001\" })", details: [], metadata: MetadataMap { headers: {"content-type": "application/grpc", "date": "Sun, 12 Apr 2026 11:55:06 GMT", "content-length": "0"} }


**Env (please complete the following information):**
- Openraft version: 0.10.0.alpha17
- Does the bug still there in the latest version?(`main` for the latest release branch, or `release-0.7` for 0.7 branch)? yes
- Rust-toolchain: cargo 1.94.1 (29ea6fb6a 2026-03-24)
- OS: Linux
- CPU: x86_64

**Additional files:**
- Logs: 
```

=== metrics after init: MetricsResponse { membership: Some(Membership { configs: [NodeIdSet { node_ids: {1: ()} }], nodes: {1: Node { node_id: 1, rpc_addr: "127.0.0.1:21001" }} }), other_metrics: "Metrics{id:1, Leader, term:1, vote:<T1-N1:Q>, last_log:1, committed:1.1, last_applied:1.1, leader:1(quorum_acked_time:14:13:09.437659, 1.234057ms ago), membership:{log_id:0.0, {voters:[{1:Node { node_id: 1, rpc_addr: \"127.0.0.1:21001\" }}], learners:[]}}, snapshot:None, purged:None, replication:{1:1.1}, heartbeat:{1:14:13:09.437457}}" }
=== Add node 2, 3 to the cluster as learners, to let them start to receive log replication from the leader
=== add-learner 2
=== add-learner 3
=== metrics after add-learner: MetricsResponse { membership: Some(Membership { configs: [NodeIdSet { node_ids: {1: ()} }], nodes: {1: Node { node_id: 1, rpc_addr: "127.0.0.1:21001" }, 2: Node { node_id: 2, rpc_addr: "127.0.0.1:21002" }, 3: Node { node_id: 3, rpc_addr: "127.0.0.1:21003" }} }), other_metrics: "Metrics{id:1, Leader, term:1, vote:<T1-N1:Q>, last_log:3, committed:1.3, last_applied:1.3, leader:1(quorum_acked_time:14:13:09.442380, 804.56µs ago), membership:{log_id:1.3, {voters:[{1:Node { node_id: 1, rpc_addr: \"127.0.0.1:21001\" }}], learners:[2:Node { node_id: 2, rpc_addr: \"127.0.0.1:21002\" },3:Node { node_id: 3, rpc_addr: \"127.0.0.1:21003\" }]}}, snapshot:None, purged:None, replication:{1:1.3,2:None,3:None}, heartbeat:{1:14:13:09.442244,2:None,3:None}}" }
=== change-membership to 1,2,3
=== metrics after change-member: MetricsResponse { membership: Some(Membership { configs: [NodeIdSet { node_ids: {1: (), 2: (), 3: ()} }], nodes: {1: Node { node_id: 1, rpc_addr: "127.0.0.1:21001" }, 2: Node { node_id: 2, rpc_addr: "127.0.0.1:21002" }, 3: Node { node_id: 3, rpc_addr: "127.0.0.1:21003" }} }), other_metrics: "Metrics{id:1, Leader, term:1, vote:<T1-N1:Q>, last_log:5, committed:1.5, last_applied:1.5, leader:1(quorum_acked_time:14:13:09.449925, 854.013µs ago), membership:{log_id:1.5, {voters:[{1:Node { node_id: 1, rpc_addr: \"127.0.0.1:21001\" },2:Node { node_id: 2, rpc_addr: \"127.0.0.1:21002\" },3:Node { node_id: 3, rpc_addr: \"127.0.0.1:21003\" }}], learners:[]}}, snapshot:None, purged:None, replication:{1:1.5,2:1.5,3:1.5}, heartbeat:{1:14:13:09.450425,2:14:13:09.449865,3:14:13:09.449925}}" }

"/rustc/e408947bfd200af42db322daf0fadfe7e26d3bd1/library/std/src/panicking.rs", line: 544 }, { fn: "std::panic::catch_unwind", file: "/rustc/e408947bfd200af42db322daf0fadfe7e26d3bd1/library/std/src/panic.rs", line: 359 }, { fn: "std::thread::lifecycle::spawn_unchecked::{{closure}}", file: "/rustc/e408947bfd200af42db322daf0fadfe7e26d3bd1/library/std/src/thread/lifecycle.rs", line: 89 }, { fn: "core::ops::function::FnOnce::call_once{{vtable.shim}}", file: "/rustc/e408947bfd200af42db322daf0fadfe7e26d3bd1/library/core/src/ops/function.rs", line: 250 }, { fn: "<alloc::boxed::Box<F,A> as core::ops::function::FnOnce<Args>>::call_once", file: "/rustc/e408947bfd200af42db322daf0fadfe7e26d3bd1/library/alloc/src/boxed.rs", line: 2206 }, { fn: "std::sys::thread::unix::Thread::new::thread_start", file: "/rustc/e408947bfd200af42db322daf0fadfe7e26d3bd1/library/std/src/sys/thread/unix.rs", line: 130 }, { fn: "start_thread", file: "./nptl/pthread_create.c", line: 448 }, { fn: "__GI___clone3", file: "./misc/../sysdeps/unix/sysv/linux/x86_64/clone3.S", line: 78 }]
```


**Additional context**
In a real life either the client must be smart to connect to the leader by default or the service implementation should be able to forward requests to the leader itself.

title: Runtime statistics collection panics
author: schreter
body:
After upgrading to the newest `openraft` main, I consistently get panics in this form:

```
    thread '<unnamed>' (80836707) panicked at .../.cargo/git/checkouts/openraft-c9fbe90779948994/aae2eea/openraft/src/base/range_map.rs:68:13:

      22:        0x102bc8300 - <openraft[258d925ee6271c57]::core::runtime_stats::runtime_stats::RuntimeStats<raft_types[4d542e7a11607d4e]::config::CSConfig<...>::record_log_stage_now
                                   at .../.cargo/git/checkouts/openraft-c9fbe90779948994/aae2eea/openraft/src/core/runtime_stats/runtime_stats.rs:168:14
      23:        0x1025953e4 - <openraft[258d925ee6271c57]::core::raft_core::RaftCore<raft_types[4d542e7a11607d4e]::config::CSConfig<...>, raft_network[4419e3111c63c2ef]::network_factory::NetworkFactory<...>, raft_storage[e1b9b65ec7d09c69]::storage::LogStorage<...>, raft_storage[e1b9b65ec7d09c69]::storage::StateMachine<...>> as openraft[258d925ee6271c57]::runtime::RaftRuntime<raft_types[4d542e7a11607d4e]::config::CSConfig<...>, raft_storage[e1b9b65ec7d09c69]::storage::StateMachine<...>::run_command::{closure#0}

                                   at .../.cargo/git/checkouts/openraft-c9fbe90779948994/aae2eea/openraft/src/core/raft_core.rs:308:40
      30:        0x10259355c - <openraft[258d925ee6271c57]::core::raft_core::RaftCore<raft_types[4d542e7a11607d4e]::config::CSConfig<...>, raft_network[4419e3111c63c2ef]::network_factory::NetworkFactory<...>, raft_storage[e1b9b65ec7d09c69]::storage::LogStorage<...>, raft_storage[e1b9b65ec7d09c69]::storage::StateMachine<...>::do_main::{closure#0}
                                   at .../.cargo/git/checkouts/openraft-c9fbe90779948994/aae2eea/openraft/src/core/raft_core.rs:296:5
      31:        0x1025d5060 - <tracing[d14a6d8d7963c731]::instrument::Instrumented<<openraft[258d925ee6271c57]::core::raft_core::RaftCore<raft_types[4d542e7a11607d4e]::config::CSConfig<...>, raft_network[4419e3111c63c2ef]::network_factory::NetworkFactory<...>, raft_storage[e1b9b65ec7d09c69]::storage::LogStorage<...>, raft_storage[e1b9b65ec7d09c69]::storage::StateMachine<...>::do_main::{closure#0}> as core[8abbb8f79ad13dba]::future::future::Future>::poll
                                   at .../.cargo/registry/src/index.crates.io-1949cf8c6b5b557f/tracing-0.1.44/src/instrument.rs:321:15
      32:        0x102591d68 - <openraft[258d925ee6271c57]::core::raft_core::RaftCore<raft_types[4d542e7a11607d4e]::config::CSConfig<...>, raft_network[4419e3111c63c2ef]::network_factory::NetworkFactory<...>, raft_storage[e1b9b65ec7d09c69]::storage::LogStorage<...>, raft_storage[e1b9b65ec7d09c69]::storage::StateMachine<...>::main::{closure#0}
                                   at .../.cargo/git/checkouts/openraft-c9fbe90779948994/aae2eea/openraft/src/core/raft_core.rs:268:62
      33:        0x1025d4f88 - <tracing[d14a6d8d7963c731]::instrument::Instrumented<<openraft[258d925ee6271c57]::core::raft_core::RaftCore<raft_types[4d542e7a11607d4e]::config::CSConfig<...>, raft_network[4419e3111c63c2ef]::network_factory::NetworkFactory<...>, raft_storage[e1b9b65ec7d09c69]::storage::LogStorage<...>, raft_storage[e1b9b65ec7d09c69]::storage::StateMachine<...>::main::{closure#0}> as core[8abbb8f79ad13dba]::future::future::Future>::poll
                                   at .../.cargo/registry/src/index.crates.io-1949cf8c6b5b557f/tracing-0.1.44/src/instrument.rs:321:15
```

**To Reproduce**

Run a workload with the newest `openraft`, I guess?

Our workload causing this is quite simple - it's basically a persistent K/V store with CRUD in an in-memory simulated cluster environment with 3 nodes (i.e., **the latencies are very low**).

Commenting out the `runtime-stats` feature mitigates the problem (at the cost of not collecting statistics).

**Env (please complete the following information):**
- Openraft version `2c4969e8f5c69a0288f44839a448b9d8e4eb9b57`
- Is the bug still there in the latest version? It seems to be only in `main`.
- Rust-toolchain: 1.94.1
- OS: MacOS 26.4 as well as Linux SLES 15 SP6
- CPU: m3 and x86_64

title: Raft log purged on leader when follower is install snapshot
author: zhixinwen
body:
Hello,

I am using `0.10.0-alpha.15`.

My application has large snapshot of several hundreds of Gbs and it takes a long time to send/install the snapshot. I notice when follower is installing the snapshot with last index 100, the leader may purge indexes past index 100 (e.g. 200). As a result, once follower is up with index 100, it cannot stream raft logs from leader. Looks like snapshot does not prevent leader from purging logs.

Please correct me if I am wrong in the understanding. Is this an issue of openraft or the application should handle it?
